system_prompt:
You are extracting load-bearing passages from one chapter of a guitar method book.

The user will give you the verbatim text of one chapter. Extract every passage
that does any of the following:

- teaches a technique (how to play, fingering, hand position, picking pattern)
- defines a concept (a chord type, a scale, a position system, a terminology)
- gives a rule (when to do X, what to avoid, what to prefer)
- shows a load-bearing example (an exercise that illustrates a method's
  characteristic move)

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  { "quotes": [
      { "topic": "<short topic label>",
        "verbatim_quote": "<exact substring of the input>",
        "why_load_bearing": "<one sentence on why this passage matters>",
        "page": <int, optional>
      },
      ...
  ] }

CRITICAL RULES:
1. `verbatim_quote` MUST be an EXACT substring of the input text. Do not
   paraphrase. Do not add words. Do not fix typos. Copy character-for-character.
2. If you can't find a substantial passage in the input that fits a topic,
   skip the topic — don't fabricate.
3. Skip prose that is purely biographical, motivational, or transitional.
   Keep prose that teaches.
4. Aim for 3-15 quotes per chapter. A chapter with only one load-bearing
   passage gets one quote; a dense chapter gets many.
5. `topic` is a short label (2-6 words). Use the book's own terminology
   when possible.
6. `why_load_bearing` is one sentence in YOUR voice explaining the
   significance — not a paraphrase of the quote.
user_prompt:
Chapter 4: The Primacy of the Melody and Its Relation to Chords
Pages 6-8 of the book.

<<<CHAPTER TEXT

exceptionally enlightening since Bernstein demonstrated his approach to learning a tune while
simultaneously teaching me a new piece of music.

During some of our lessons, I would bring in my original compositions, which were heavily
influenced by jazz standards. Most of them followed the typical 32-bar structures with AABA or
ABAC forms, using common jazz chord progressions. Occasionally, I would venture into more
unconventional harmonic territories, but the overall style remained traditional. I was curious to
see how Bernstein would approach learning and interpreting new music, and the way he
approached it was very insightful.

Initially, he would take a somewhat conservative approach to the chord voicings, mostly sticking
to basic shell voicings, with occasional signature chord runs where he knew they would work.
However, as we progressed through the tune, after playing the third or fourth chorus, he would
begin to explore further. He would introduce more extended chords, harmonic substitutions, and
experiment with rhythm and textures. By the end of the tune, it would sound as if he had been
practicing it for years.

I noticed that by that point he had a complete understanding of how the tune was constructed
harmonically and melodically. He could identify the structural notes of the melody and
understand how the melody and harmony interacted. I noticed this because Bernstein would
provide me with suggestions on how to make it more interesting and align it with the thousands
of great tunes he knows. He would often demonstrate different approaches by playing sections of
the tune himself, combining chords and melody in a solo guitar arrangement. He would then
showcase alternative ways to harmonize a melody note or present different compositional
pathways he would have explored.


The primacy of the melody

Studying with Bernstein has revealed to me his mastery of the fundamentals. He possesses a
keen sense of what is important and essential in a tune, with the melody standing out as a crucial
element.

I'm constantly impressed by the level of detail he employs when playing the melody. His choice
of phrasing and articulation is always deeply informed by the original versions of the tune,
particularly in hard bop compositions where the melody's phrasing is often fixed due to multiple
instruments playing in unison or harmonizing the main melody. Moreover, his ability to translate
the nuances of horn interpretations to the guitar, as we explored in the technique chapter, is well
thought out.



                                                                                                  6


During one lesson, I shared a solo guitar arrangement of Charles Mingus' blues tune, "Nostalgia
in Times Square." However, I wasn't satisfied with how it sounded as I attempted to play the
melody and chords simultaneously. Bernstein interrupted me and emphasized that the most
important thing, regardless of the setting, is to never sacrifice the phrasing of the melody. He
proceeded to demonstrate a beautiful example, articulating the melody and playing the chords
during the spaces suggested by the melody. His approach effectively conveyed the essence of the
tune.

He explained that some guitarists try to play everything simultaneously, but in doing so, they
sacrifice the feel and phrasing, resulting in stiffened music. Instead, he appreciates players who
infuse feeling into the notes of the melody, rather than, as he put it, "trying to juggle while riding
an unicycle."

On another occasion, Bernstein asked me to perform a solo piece, and I chose "The Days of
Wine and Roses." While playing, I inadvertently forgot a part of the melody and substituted it
with chord movements to fill the gap. He stopped me and pointed out that I was trying to rush to
step 12 without adequately accomplishing step 1, which is mastering the melody. Drawing again
a parallel to the analogy of a play or a movie, for him trying to play a chord run without honoring
the melody is like a car chase without proper context within the story.

For Bernstein, the initial step in learning a song is to be able to play the melody as a monophonic
instrument and accurately deliver the story. He also emphasized the importance of the
relationship between the melody and lyrics, noting how understanding the words helps to grasp
the connection between syllables and notes.


The relation between chords and melody

As I mentioned earlier in this chapter, Bernstein would teach me new tunes using an oral
approach rather than relying on lead sheets. One of the main points he emphasized during these
sessions was the significance of understanding the relationship between the melody and the
chords.

To teach me a song, he would start by playing a solo take where he clearly played the melody
and rhythms. This allowed me to hear the tune before he began teaching me section by section. If
the melody of the tune was straightforward, he would teach me the chords and melody
simultaneously, always highlighting the importance of comprehending their connection.

For instance, during one lesson, he taught me "The End of a Love Affair." The first chord in the
tune is a Gm7, and the melody features an A note, which is the 9th of the Gm7 chord. Instead of
directly telling me what that was, he played that little section and asked me what I heard. I

                                                                                                     7


played a Gm9 chord with the 9th on top, and he described it as a "flavor" or a "spice." According
to him, one should know exactly what that spice will add to the mix. He also explained that an
alternative way to approach that section is by playing a D7sus with an A on top, which would be
a variation of the original flavor—still "spicy," but "a little bit more bitter." Bernstein thinks
about chords and tensions in terms of relating them to the senses and something more
memorable, rather than as a collection of abstract names and numbers. This way, he can hear
certain chords and associate them with similar flavors, just like how one can find the same spice
but with different nuances of freshness or sweetness.

On another occasion, he taught me the song "Simone" by Frank Foster, which features a more
active melody and a faster tempo. For this song, he first taught me the chord progression, and
then he had me play the chords while he played the melody. The goal was for me to hear the
complete song and grasp the rhythms of the melody. Afterward, he taught me the melody. At this
stage, he suggested that I think about the notes that fit the chord while figuring out the melody by
ear.

Moreover, when the melody targeted a specific note of the chord, he encouraged me to take note
of it as something to remember and as a guideline for deciding which types of chords to use. For
example, in the harmony of this particular tune, which resembles a minor blues in D, an A7
chord always resolves to D minor. In such contexts, where a minor I (one) chord serves as the
tonic of the song, Bernstein explained that it's usually more appropriate to play a Dm6 chord,
where the major 6th replaces the minor 7th within the chord. However, in this particular song,
the melody plays the minor 7th of the chord, so he suggested playing more of a Dm7-type chord,
possibly using quartal voicings to emphasize the 7th and the 11th.

For Bernstein, it is essential to learn the melody and chords together or, at the very least, to
combine them as soon as possible to truly grasp the meaning and construction of the song.


Language, rhythm and meaning

Whenever Bernstein taught me a song or a phrase, he always encouraged me to pay close
attention to the rhythm after learning the notes. He believed that rhythm gives meaning to the
musical idea. While the notes are certainly a part of it, it is the way they fit within the measure
and the spaces between them that imbues those notes with significance as an idea. He drew an
analogy to language and punctuation, explaining that saying "Hello, nice to see you" is different
from saying "Hello nice to, see you." The former makes sense, while the latter is awkward and
unclear in meaning.




                                                                                                      8


CHAPTER TEXT>>>
